questions.prompt: Complete: "I ___ play outside until dark when I was a kid."
questions.explanation: Playing outside is a repeated past habit that no longer happens, so "used to" is correct: I used to play outside. "am/was used to" and "get used to" mean accustomed to, which is a different idea.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My grandmother ___ bake bread every Sunday morning."
questions.explanation: Baking bread is a repeated past action (not a state), so "would" fits perfectly: she would bake bread every Sunday. "got/is used to" means accustomed to and takes -ing. "used to be" adds "be" which doesn't fit before "bake".

questions.prompt: Complete: "He ___ have long hair when he was a student."
questions.explanation: "have" (possess) is a state verb, so "would" cannot be used — only "used to" works: He used to have long hair. "was/is used to" means accustomed to, not a past habit.

In questions with did, the -d disappears: Did you use to walk to school? This is the standard question form. "used to" keeps the -d which is wrong after did, and "using to" / "would" do not fit this question structure.

"get used to + -ing" means to become accustomed to something: she is getting used to living alone. "used to" alone describes a past habit (finished), and "would" describes repeated past actions, neither of which fits this present-progressive context.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We ___ spend our summers at the beach when the children were small."
questions.explanation: Spending summers at the beach is a past repeated habit now finished, so "used to" is correct: We used to spend our summers at the beach. "are/got used to" means accustomed to, and "use to" is only correct after did in a question.
